items.foco: Classic triad: pain, vomiting, jelly stools
items.title: classical triad
items.contentHtml:
Classic triad

  • Intermittent colic abdominal pain (the child draws in his legs) is the earliest symptom (90% of cases).
  • Bileous vomiting appears in advanced stages and suggests proximal intestinal obstruction.
  • Currant jelly stools (blood + mucus) are late and they translate ischemia of the invaginated mucosa; They are not necessary for the diagnosis.
  • Only 15-20% present the complete triad on admission; The absence does not rule out intussusception.
  • Pain precedes vomiting and rectal bleeding; The complete triad is highly specific but not very sensitive.

items.title: Ultrasound: pathognomonic signs
items.contentHtml:
Ultrasound: pathognomonic signs

  • Target sign (or doughnut sign) in cross section: external hypoechoic ring (edema of the intestinal wall) and hyperechoic center (trapped mucosa and mesentery).
  • Pseudo-kidney sign in longitudinal section: image of ectopic kidney with the mesentery as an echogenic “pelvis” and the intestinal walls as hypoechoic “parenchyma”.
  • Abdominal ultrasound with a high-frequency linear probe (7.5-12 MHz) has sensitivity >95% and specificity >98%, making it the first-line test.
  • Additional findings: free fluid between the loops suggests advanced ischemia or drilling; absence of vascularization on Doppler color indicates necrosis and contraindicates reduction by enema.

items.title: typical age
items.contentHtml:
Typical age

  • The peak incidence occurs between 3 months and 3 years, with a maximum between 6 and 12 months.
  • In children under 3 months, intussusception is usually secondary to Meckel's diverticulum or polyp; in people over 3 years of age juvenile polyp, lymphoma or Henoch-Schönlein purpura are suspected.
  • The male:female ratio is 3:1.
  • Outside the typical age range, the probability of an underlying organic cause (pathological reference point) is greater and requires lesions to be ruled out with imaging studies or surgery.

items.foco: Most common cause: idiopathic or lymphoid hyperplasia
items.title: Most common cause
items.contentHtml:
Most common cause

  • In 90% of children between 3 months and 3 years, intussusception is idiopathic or is associated with lymphoid hyperplasia (hypertrophy of Peyer's patches) after viral infections (adenovirus, rotavirus).
  • Lymphoid hyperplasia acts as a “fold” that is dragged by the peristalsis, generating invagination.
  • The rotavirus vaccine (old formulation) was associated with a higher risk; Current vaccines have not shown a significant increase.
  • In children over 3 years of age or with recurrence, the cause is usually an organic lesion (polyp, Meckel's diverticulum, lymphoma, intestinal duplication).

items.title: Anatomical landmark
items.contentHtml:
Anatomical landmark

  • The majority of intussusceptions (85%) are ileocolic, where the terminal ileum invaginates through the ileocecal valve into the ascending colon.
  • The ileocecal valve acts as a point of fixation; the mesentery of the ileum is dragged, compressing vessels and causing edema and venous obstruction.
  • Less common types: ileoileal, colic-colic and jejuno-jejunal (the latter usually have a pathological reference point such as a polyp or Meckel's diverticulum).
  • The ileocolic location explains the sign of Dance (emptiness in the right iliac fossa on palpation).

items.foco: Barium enema as diagnosis and treatment
items.title: Barium enema: diagnostic and therapeutic
items.contentHtml:
Barium enema: diagnostic and therapeutic

  • The barium enema (or enema with air/water-soluble contrast) is both diagnostic and therapeutic: when the contrast refluxes, the image of “stack of coins” or “spiral spring” is observed at the point of obstruction.
  • Hydrostatic pressure (barium at 1 meter high) or pneumatic (air at 80-120 mmHg) reduces intussusception in 80-95% of cases.
  • It is considered successful when the contrast refluxes freely to the terminal ileum and the mass disappears.
  • The success rate is higher if the procedure is performed within the first 24 hours of symptoms.
  • The air enema (pneumatic) is preferred due to less radiation and lower risk of perforation with chemical peritonitis.

items.title: Enema contraindication
items.contentHtml:
Contraindication of enema

  • Intestinal perforation is an absolute contraindication for reduction by enema (barium, air or water-soluble contrast).
  • Signs of perforation: pneumoperitoneum on plain abdominal radiography (standing or lateral decubitus), abundant free fluid with pneumoperitoneum on ultrasound, or clinical signs of peritonitis (abdominal rigidity, pain on decompression, high fever, extreme leukocytosis).
  • If perforation is suspected, urgent exploratory laparotomy with surgical reduction and resection if there is necrosis is indicated.
  • Hemodynamic instability and irreversible ischemia (absence of Doppler flow) also contraindicate the enema and require surgery.

items.title: surgical reduction
items.contentHtml:
Surgical reduction if enema fails

  • If reduction by enema fails (fails to deinvaginate after 3 attempts) or is contraindicated, laparotomy or laparoscopy is performed.
  • In surgery, manual deinvagination is performed using gentle pressure from the distal to the proximal segment; The proximal intestine should not be pulled.
  • If the intestine presents necrosis (blackish color, absence of peristalsis, lack of bleeding when cut), resection of the segment with primary anastomosis is performed.
  • The recurrence rate after surgical reduction is lower (<2%) than after reduction by enema (5-10%).
  • During surgery, the should explore the presence of a pathological reference point (Meckel's diverticulum, polyp, duplication).

items.foco: Possible cause: Meckel's polyp or diverticulum
items.title: Organic causes: Meckel's polyp and diverticulum
items.contentHtml:
Possible cause: Meckel's polyp or diverticulum

  • The Meckel's diverticulum is the most common organic cause of intussusception in children >3 years (and in children younger than 3 months), especially when it invaginates as the head of the intussusceptum segment.
  • Juvenile polyps (hamartomatous) are another cause; They are usually located in the terminal ileum and act as a reference point.
  • Other causes: non-Hodgkin lymphoma (especially in children >6 years), Henoch-Schönlein purpura (due to mural hematoma), intestinal duplication and duplication cyst.
  • The presence of recurrence or intussusception in atypical age requires these lesions to be ruled out using ultrasound, computed tomography or laparoscopy.

items.foco: Dance sign: void in the right iliac fossa
items.title: Dance sign
items.contentHtml:
Dance's sign

  • The Dance sign consists of the sensation of emptiness or depression upon deep palpation of the right iliac fossa.
  • It occurs because the terminal ileum and the cecum have invaginated into the ascending colon, moving out of their usual position.
  • It is a clinical sign insensitive (present in <30% of cases) but highly specific when detected in a child with suspected intussusception.
  • It is classically associated with the ileocolic form; It is not observed in ileoileal or colic-colic intussusceptions.
  • Palpation of an elongated sausage-shaped mass in the right hypochondrium or epigastrium is another clinical finding (sign of the abdominal mass).

items.foco: Free fluid on ultrasound suggests ischemia
items.title: Free fluid on ultrasound
items.contentHtml:
Free fluid on ultrasound suggests ischemia

  • The presence of free fluid between the intestinal loops on ultrasound is a marker of advanced intestinal ischemia and possible necrosis.
  • The fluid is generally anechoic (transudate) in the initial stages, but may become echogenic if there is hemorrhagic content due to infarction.
  • Other signs ultrasound of ischemia: wall thickening (>8 mm), absence of vascularization on color Doppler, thickened folds and increased echogenicity of the mesentery.
  • The presence of significant free fluid (especially with pneumoperitoneum) contraindicates reduction by enema and requires urgent surgery.

items.title: Recurrence after non-surgical reduction
items.contentHtml:
Recurrence: 5-10% after non-surgical reduction

  • The recurrence rate after successful reduction by enema (barium or air) is 5-10%, generally within the first 72 hours.
  • Early recurrence (<24 hours) is usually due to incomplete reduction or residual edema; late (>1 week) requires ruling out an underlying organic lesion.
  • If the recurrence is unique and the child is stable, the enema can be repeated; if there are >2 recurrences or atypical age, exploratory laparoscopy is recommended.
  • Recurrence after surgical reduction is <2%.
  • Cecofixation (cecopexy) is not routinely recommended to prevent recurrences.
